Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can start at 7.05 per unit for this volume, which is in line with where supply is trading and gives us a solid basis to commit capacity. If you can move close to that level, I’m open to working through the remaining terms quickly and aiming for a dependable supply arrangement. There is no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable first move is to anchor with a sell offer. The latest market reference is 6.74, so opening modestly above that supports the supplier objective while leaving room to negotiate and preserve volume.

Thanks for the opening. That level is well above where this purchase works for our production economics, even allowing for current market firmness, so I need to start materially lower at 2.68 per unit. If there’s flexibility on your side and you can move down substantially, I’m willing to keep discussing volume and timing to see whether we can narrow the gap. The supplier opened far above the buyer’s workable range, so accepting is not reasonable. A low counteranchor preserves negotiating room and signals a serious pricing constraint without ending the discussion.

Thanks for the movement, but 6.62 is still well outside a level that works for us on a buy-side basis. We can improve modestly to 2.80 per unit as a good-faith step, and if there is meaningful flexibility from your side we can continue discussing volume commitments and timing to help bridge the gap. At your current number, the economics simply do not leave enough room for us downstream. The supplier moved down only slightly relative to a very large gap, so continuing with a small upward concession is the most reasonable way to keep the negotiation alive without giving away too much too early. Even though the market reference is high, the current seller offer remains far from a workable buying level for the manufacturer, so a cautious counter is appropriate.

I appreciate the good-faith move to 2.80, but that level is still far below anything we can support given current market conditions and the value in this supply. We have already come down from our prior position, and to keep this discussion moving I can reduce further to 5.95 per unit. If you can make a more meaningful step up from your side, we can also discuss volume commitment and delivery timing to help firm up the overall package. The buyer's offer is well above my floor but still dramatically below both my latest ask and the observed market reference, so accepting would leave too much value on the table. A continued counteroffer is the most reasonable move because there is still room and time to negotiate, and a moderate concession signals flexibility without collapsing too quickly.

Thanks for the movement, but 5.95 is still well outside a workable range for us on this program. We can improve from our 2.80 and come to 3.05 per unit if we can align on a practical volume and dependable delivery schedule. That is a real step on our side and reflects our need to protect downstream margin while still trying to keep the discussion productive. The supplier's ask remains far above our current buying range, so accepting would undermine our economics. A measured counter keeps the negotiation alive, shows some flexibility, and tests whether the seller is serious about narrowing the gap.

I appreciate the movement to 3.05, but that level is still too far below a workable supply position for us, especially with the market reference sitting materially higher. To keep this moving, I can make a meaningful concession from 5.95 and come down to 5.20 per unit if we can pair it with committed volume and a dependable delivery schedule.
